data.title: Oil prices impact the Federal Reserve—has the Bitcoin cycle truly been broken?
data.description:
I. The Divergence in Market Narratives

Bitcoin has been consolidating above $77,000 for some time. Market sentiment regarding the second half of the year is severely divided.

Optimists believe that the $60,000 level in February was the bottom of this bear market, and that a steady recovery will occur in the second half of the year. Cautious observers warn that new lows are expected, with the true bottom potentially at $55,000 or even lower. Pessimists predict that the bear market will extend into 2027.

Each side has its own opinion, making it difficult to reach a consensus.

A deeper problem lies in the divergence of methodologies. Some believe that the four-year cycle remains valid, and everything is within the established patterns.

Some believe the cycle has been broken, requiring a completely new analytical framework. Others only look at macroeconomic data, believing that oil prices and the Federal Reserve determine everything. ChainChain believes that these debates fail to reach a consensus because they lack a unified methodology. Let's return to a basic philosophical framework: internal factors are the basis of the development of things, external factors are the conditions for the development of things, and external factors act through internal factors. Using this framework to view Bitcoin, many things become clearer. II. What are the Internal Factors of Bitcoin? Internal factors are the inherent contradictions within things that determine their essence and development trend. Bitcoin's internal factors include at least four levels. The first layer is the fundamental contradiction: the contradiction between the rigidity of supply caused by the halving and the price elasticity of demand. This is the underlying driving force of the four-year cycle. The halving of mining rewards every four years changes the market's supply and demand structure from the supply side. This mechanism is written into the code and will not change due to fluctuations in oil prices. The second layer is the techno-economic contradiction: the contradiction between computing power costs and coin prices. Miners invest energy and hardware in mining, and costs determine their behavior. When the coin price is lower than the cost, inefficient miners are forced to shut down, computing power decreases, and then the market rebalances. The third layer is the internal market contradiction: the contradiction between long-term holders and short-term speculators. LTH (Long Term Holders) are responsible for accumulation and distribution, while STH (Short Term Holders) are responsible for providing liquidity. When chips shift from strong to weak hands, the market peaks; when they shift from weak to strong hands, the market bottoms out. On-chain metrics like MVRV and SOPR measure this contradictory state. The fourth layer is the contradiction between narrative and reality: Bitcoin is touted as digital gold and an inflation hedge, but its actual price movements are often highly correlated with the Nasdaq. This contradiction determines Bitcoin's resilience under different macroeconomic environments. These four layers of internal factors constitute the cyclical framework of Bitcoin, relatively independent of the macroeconomic environment. They do not disappear due to fluctuations in oil prices. A counterexample can prove the existence of these internal factors. In 2023, the Federal Reserve was still in a tightening cycle, and the macroeconomic environment was unfavorable. However, Bitcoin rose from $16,000 to over $70,000. What drives this market is the halving expectation and the ETF narrative—these are all internal factors. If the external determinism holds true, the extremely loose macroeconomic environment from 2020 to 2021 should have led to a one-sided rise in Bitcoin. In fact, it has experienced several rounds of corrections of more than 30%. Therefore, internal factors are the basis. External factors are the conditions. III. The current oil price shock: how external factors work. Cryptoslate published an article on April 25 analyzing the impact of the oil price shock on the Federal Reserve and Bitcoin[1]. The article's logical chain is: tensions in the Strait of Hormuz led to oil supply disruptions, rising oil prices pushed up inflation, the Federal Reserve was forced to maintain a hawkish stance, and Bitcoin came under pressure. ChainChain believes the value of this analysis lies in accurately identifying the most important external variable. However, its treatment of causality is too linear, easily leading to the misconception that oil prices directly determine Bitcoin's price fluctuations. Let's look at the facts first. On April 20th, shipping in the Strait of Hormuz almost came to a standstill. After warning shots were fired and an Iranian cargo ship was seized, only a very small number of ships passed through in 12 hours. The normal throughput is approximately 130 ships per day. St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem stated that high oil prices could keep core inflation around 3%, well above the 2% target. New York Fed President John Williams said the situation in the Middle East is already pushing up inflationary pressures and increasing uncertainty. The Fed will hold its FOMC meeting on April 28-29, and will release Q1 GDP and PCE data on April 30. These events, all within three days, require the market to simultaneously digest new inflation concerns, the Fed's statements, and key economic data. These facts are clear. However, the problem lies in the fact that the transmission path from oil prices to Bitcoin is not a straight line. Oil prices do not directly push down Bitcoin, but rather exert their influence through factors affecting Bitcoin's internal structure. Specifically: Rising oil prices increase energy costs, which will affect miners. Inefficient mines will shut down, leading to a short-term decrease in hashrate, impacting Bitcoin's security and narrative. However, efficient miners will survive, potentially increasing industry concentration. Rising inflation expectations due to oil prices will affect the narrative of Bitcoin as digital gold. If Bitcoin exhibits anti-inflationary properties in this round, the narrative will be strengthened. If it falls along with risk assets, the narrative will be weakened. Delayed interest rate cuts due to oil prices will affect the opportunity cost for short-term speculators. Decreased risk appetite leads to speculative capital outflows, increasing short-term selling pressure. The macroeconomic uncertainty brought about by oil prices will affect the behavior of long-term holders. They may accelerate their accumulation, viewing Bitcoin as a safe-haven asset; or they may reduce their holdings and switch to gold or the US dollar. Which one happens depends on observing on-chain data. Therefore, even with the same oil price shock, Bitcoin's final trend depends on the net effect of these forces interacting. Different internal conditions can lead to different results from the same external factor. This also explains why Bitcoin's performance might differ in 2022 and 2026, both years with high oil prices. In 2022, the internal factor was the second year after the halving, historically bearish, with both internal and external factors moving in the same direction and resonating downwards, resulting in a deep drop. In 2026, the internal factor added institutional buying from ETFs, offsetting some of the external pressure. IV. The Cycle Has Not Been Broken, Only Modulated Many people in the market say that the four-year cycle has been broken. ChainTeach believes this statement needs careful analysis. First, let's define what hasn't been broken. Halving still occurs every four years. Rigid changes in supply still exist. The contradiction between miners' computing power costs and coin prices continues. The exchange of tokens between LTH and STH remains the core mechanism for bull and bear market cycles. On-chain indicators such as MVRV and SOPR still exhibit similar cyclical patterns to the past. The framework of the cycle remains intact. What has truly changed is the weight of external factors. In Bitcoin's early days, the external macroeconomic environment had a weak connection to it. Price fluctuations were primarily driven by internal narratives. Now, things are different. Bitcoin's market capitalization is large enough, and its connection to the global financial system is deep enough. External variables such as the Federal Reserve's interest rate decisions, changes in global M2, and geopolitical risks have an increasingly significant impact on price. This can be understood using a physics analogy. Bitcoin's internal factor is the inherent frequency of an oscillator, with a cycle of approximately four years. Macroeconomic external factors are external driving forces, whose frequency and amplitude are constantly changing. Actual price movements are a superposition of the inherent oscillation and the external driving forces. When the frequency of an external driving force is close to the natural frequency, the amplitude is amplified. When the direction is opposite, the trend becomes tangled. When the external driving force is strong enough, the periodic characteristics of the natural frequency are masked—but not disappeared. The correct understanding of a cycle breaking is not that the cycle disappears, but that the weight of external factors increases significantly, to the point that the inherent cycle of internal factors is modulated. The amplitude and phase change, but the framework remains. This also explains a previously discussed question: why was the 2021 bull market's gains smaller than in 2017, but the 2022 bear market's losses still deep? Because the limited gains in the bull market are a result of the diminishing marginal effect of the halving narrative—this is a change at the level of internal factors. However, the depth of a bear market depends on whether internal and external factors resonate. In 2022, both internal and external factors were bearish (the second year after the halving) and aggressive interest rate hikes, resulting in a significant downward trend. The trend in 2026 will similarly depend on whether internal and external factors resonate or counteract each other. V. Three Scenarios for the Second Half of 2026 Based on the framework of internal and external factors, three scenarios can be deduced.

Scenario A: Steady Recovery

In terms of internal factors, the historical pattern of the second year after the halving is bearish, but institutional buying brought by ETFs provides structural support. In terms of external factors, oil prices fell, interest rate cut expectations were restored, and the macro environment shifted from tight to loose.

The relationship between internal and external factors is a hedge: the shift from bearish to bullish external factors hedged the bearish pressure of internal factors. The result is that $60,000 is the bottom, and the market will fluctuate upward in the second half of the year, gradually challenging the previous high.

The representative institutions for this scenario are Bernstein, VanEck and Grayscale. VanEck analysts pointed out that the Bitcoin hash rate is recovering and the funding rate is in the negative range, which are bullish technical signals[2]. Scenario B: Bottoming Out After Hitting New Lows, Recovery in 2027 Internally, the market is bearish; the pressure from the second year after the halving has not yet been fully released. On-chain indicators such as MVRV have not yet entered their historical bottom range. Externally, oil prices are stubborn, the Federal Reserve maintains a hawkish stance, and expectations for interest rate cuts are further postponed. Both internal and external factors are bearish, but not in extreme convergence. The result is that Bitcoin falls to the $55,000 to $60,000 range, completes its final drop, begins to bottom out, and a recovery is expected in 2027. This scenario is represented by some on-chain analysts at CryptoQuant. Scenario C: Bear Market Extended to 2027 Internally, besides the bearish pressure in the second year after the halving, miners also face profit pressure from rising energy costs. If the price of Bitcoin continues to be low, a large-scale shutdown wave may occur. The willingness to accumulate LTH may also be shaken by the continued decline. Externally, oil prices remain high, interest rate cuts are postponed to 2027, and an economic recession may even occur. The Federal Reserve is struggling to balance inflation and growth. The internal and external factors are converging downwards, and this is not a short-term convergence, but a prolonged one. The result is that Bitcoin will fall to $45,000 or even lower, with recovery postponed until after the second quarter of 2027. This scenario is represented by several seasoned traders and on-chain analysts. Peter Brandt believes the market is still missing a final, decisive drop. Willy Woo predicts a typical bear market bottom range of around $45,000, with $30,000 and $16,000 as deeper support levels if the macroeconomy deteriorates. Benjamin Cowen believes the four-year cycle remains intact, and it's far too early to declare the bear market over. VI. How to Observe the Balance of Power Between Internal and External Factors For investors who don't want to guess market movements, establishing an observation framework is more important. Internal factors include: whether the MVRV Z-score has entered its historical bottom range; whether LTH's net position is accumulating or distributing; whether miner hashrate and difficulty have experienced a large-scale decline; and whether short-term holders, as measured by SOPR, have engaged in panic selling. External factors include: changes in the probability of interest rate cuts shown by CME FedWatch; oil price trends and geopolitical developments in the Strait of Hormuz; and weekly fund flows of Bitcoin ETFs—these are the mediating variables through which external factors influence internal factors. The most crucial judgment is whether internal and external factors are in a state of resonance or hedging. If the resonance is upward, with both internal and external factors being bullish, the market will break out strongly. A downward resonance, with both internal and external factors weakening, will lead to a deep correction, similar to 2022. In a hedging state, with one side long and the other short, the market will exhibit consolidation, fluctuation, a slow bull market, or a slow bear market. The current market is likely somewhere in this hedging state. VII. Conclusion Returning to the question posed at the beginning of this article: Has Bitcoin's four-year cycle truly been broken? ChainChain believes the cycle has not been broken. The internal contradictions—halving, supply and demand, miners, hoarders, and speculators—are still at play. The framework of the cycle remains intact. What has truly changed is the weight of external factors. Bitcoin is no longer a marginal asset isolated from the global financial system. Its market capitalization is large enough, and its connection to the macro environment is deep enough. Oil prices, the Federal Reserve, and liquidity—these external variables have an unprecedented impact on Bitcoin. External factors work through internal factors. The same external factor can lead to different results under different internal conditions. This is the most basic principle of dialectics, but it is often forgotten in market discussions. For the second half of 2026, no one can give a definitive prediction of whether prices will rise or fall. The market is complex, and the interplay of internal and external factors presents multiple possibilities. But what is clear is that regardless of which of the three scenarios materializes, a recovery in 2027 is highly probable. Because Bitcoin's intrinsic factors won't disappear. Halvings are still happening, and scarcity is still increasing. External factors—macro liquidity—will eventually shift. The Federal Reserve can't tighten forever; the long-term trend of global M2 is expansion. Within a unified analytical framework of internal and external factors, investors can be less anxious about short-term fluctuations and more focused on the long-term structure. Instead of arguing whether 60,000 is the bottom, it's better to observe variables like oil prices, FedWatch, and ETF flows, and how they affect Bitcoin's on-chain signals. Stick to dollar-cost averaging, add to positions on dips. Adhere to a long-term perspective.

data.title: Quantum computing cracked a 15-bit private key? The truth is quite the opposite.
data.description:
BTC continued to fall slightly, reaching 77k. Whenever this happens, there are always some news items that fuel the fire. This time, it's quantum computing again.

Cointelegraph reported a news item: Researcher Giancarlo Lelli successfully cracked a 15-bit elliptic curve key using a quantum computer, and Bitcoin uses the same type of encryption algorithm. [1] As soon as the news came out, the internet exploded. Some people exclaimed that Bitcoin was going to go to zero, some people started to promote so-called anti-quantum coins, and some even directly asserted that there was not much time left for BTC.

When I saw this kind of news, my first reaction was that there was a trap.

Every year or two, quantum computing will come out to make its presence known. Every time it is the same recipe, the same taste. It's just that this time what was brought to the stage was a quantum computer that cracked a 15-bit key. It sounds very impressive, but upon closer inspection, there are several traps hidden inside.

Trap 1: 15-bit vs. 256-bit The core selling point of the report is its emphasis on similar encryption methods. The implication is that quantum computers can already crack the algorithm used by Bitcoin. But there's a crucial detail hidden here: the cracked key is 15-bit, while Bitcoin uses 256-bit. How big is this difference? Let's give you an intuitive understanding. A 15-bit key space has approximately 32,000 possibilities (haha). You can brute-force this in less than a millisecond using any laptop or even a mobile phone. This isn't a technical challenge; it's an assignment even a middle school student could complete. And how big is a 256-bit key space? It's 2 to the power of 256. How large is that number? It's orders of magnitude more than the total number of atoms in the universe. Even if all the conventional computers on Earth tried to brute-force it, from the birth of the universe to its destruction, they still wouldn't be able to guess a single correct private key. The report conflates cracking a 15-bit key with threatening a 256-bit key, like someone claiming to have broken the 100-meter world record, only to find out they only ran one meter. The principle is correct—both are running—but the practical implications are vastly different. Trap Two: The Cost of Quantum Advantage is Hidden. Some might argue that quantum computers don't rely on brute-force enumeration, but rather on the mathematical ingenuity of Shor's algorithm. Even if only 15 bits can be cracked now, it will eventually expand to 256 bits, and the time will only increase linearly. This argument is mathematically correct, but it ignores a fatal problem in physics and engineering: exploding costs. In previous articles, we have repeatedly emphasized the point that there is no free lunch. For traditional computers to crack encryption, the cost is an exponential increase in time; for quantum computers to crack encryption, the cost is not time, but an exponential increase in physical resources. [2] To crack a 15-bit key, it may require dozens of physical qubits. But to crack a 256-bit key, research institutions estimate that millions of high-quality, low-error physical qubits are needed. [3] From dozens to millions, this is not a linear increase, but a leap of four orders of magnitude. More importantly, qubits are extremely fragile and require extremely low temperature environments, complex error correction mechanisms, and near-perfect control. For every order of magnitude increase in qubits, the engineering difficulty and cost do not double, but explode. The most advanced Google Willow quantum computer currently has only 105 physical qubits. [4] From 105 to millions, the gap is not a few years, but a technological chasm. The report makes no mention of this, as if the jump from 15 bits to 256 bits is just a small step. This omission is precisely the key technique used to create panic. Trap Three: Bitcoin Isn't Naked Even if we take a step back and assume that quantum computers eventually reach that level, Bitcoin isn't completely defenseless. Satoshi Nakamoto considered this issue when designing Bitcoin. Bitcoin addresses don't store the public key itself, but rather the hash value of the public key. Hash functions have a natural resistance to quantum computing. The Grover algorithm can only reduce the difficulty of attacking the hash from 2 to the power of 256 to 2 to the power of 128, which is still an astronomical number. [5] As long as your Bitcoin exists in a P2PKH address (starting with 1) or P2WPKH address (starting with bc1q) and has never been used (i.e., the public key has not been exposed), then the quantum computer is facing this hash shell, not the fragile elliptic curve signature. As Satoshi Nakamoto said in 2010: Transactions paid to Bitcoin addresses are only as secure as hashes. [6] This is an easily overlooked but extremely important technical detail. It was not mentioned in the report because it would not be alarming enough. Trap Four: Bitcoin is alive. Finally, and most importantly: Bitcoin is not dead. It can be upgraded. The threat of quantum computing was not discovered today; the Bitcoin community began researching solutions many years ago. The Taproot upgrade activated in 2021 has paved the way for future changes to the signature algorithm. [7] Research on quantum-resistant signatures is also underway, but the current solutions are not mature enough. The main problem is that the signature size is too large, which will cause the Bitcoin block to expand by hundreds or even thousands of times, which violates the fundamental reason why Satoshi Nakamoto chose ECC instead of RSA. When quantum computing really approaches the threat threshold, the Bitcoin community can initiate a soft fork to change the signature algorithm to a quantum-resistant version. This process will not be more complicated than the Taproot upgrade in 2021. In contrast, traditional banking systems, internet security systems, and military communication systems face more direct and serious quantum threats, and are more difficult to upgrade. If quantum computers are ever put into practical use, the first headache will definitely not be for Bitcoin holders, but for central banks, the Pentagon, and global cybersecurity engineers.

Behind the Panic

Since the logic is so clear, why is there a wave of quantum panic every now and then?

Chain Education observes that there are probably three types of promoters behind it.

The first type is the media. The title "Quantum computing cracks encryption" is naturally easy to spread. As for whether it is 15 bits or 256 bits that are cracked, whether it is laboratory conditions or real environment, these details are too boring to be included in the title. Click rate is the most important thing, and the truth can be put later.

The second type is people who use panic to sell products. Every time quantum panic strikes, there are always some project teams that jump out and sell so-called anti-quantum coins. Chain Education wrote an article in June last year with the title "Beware of anti-quantum scams". [8] These projects are usually technically terrible, but the concepts are packaged in a dazzling way, and they are specifically designed to harvest retail investors in panic. In such situations, simply blocking someone is likely either foolish or malicious. The third type is ordinary investors swept up in emotions. They panic at the sight of news headlines, without time to read the full article, let alone verify the technical details. Panic is especially amplified during market downturns, creating a self-reinforcing negative cycle. Prepare for the future, not be overly worried. This article by ChainChain is not intended to deny the threat of quantum computing. The quantum threat is a real, long-term risk that we cannot ignore. However, the correct attitude towards risk is to prepare for the future, not to be led by the nose by panic. For ordinary Bitcoin holders, there are a few simple things to do now to reduce future quantum risks. First, check your address type. Use P2PKH (starting with 1) or P2WPKH (starting with bc1q) addresses whenever possible, and avoid using P2PK (early addresses that directly expose public keys) or P2TR (Taproot addresses starting with bc1p, which also expose public key variants). [5] Second, avoid address reuse. Each address should ideally be used only once. If you need to access the Bitcoin in an address, transfer it all at once, clear the address, and never use it again. This will minimize the time window for public key exposure. Third, don't wait until the last day to act. If a large number of users start migrating in the future, on-chain transaction fees will definitely skyrocket. It is wise to take precautions while the network is calm now. As for those who use quantum panic to scare people every day and then promote all sorts of messy projects, please remember one sentence: quantum computers have not yet arrived, but their sickles have already been exposed. Bitcoin has faced countless threats of destruction. From government bans to mining crashes, from 51% attacks to fork wars, each time someone predicted Bitcoin would go to zero. But each time, Bitcoin has survived. Quantum computing will not be the end of Bitcoin. It is simply another test in its long history. And Bitcoin's design was prepared for such a test from the very beginning. Instead of being swept away by panic, it's better to calm down, understand the technology, see the logic, and make rational judgments.

data.title: DeepSeek V4 makes a stunning debut: Ten first principles for competing with its rivals.
data.description:
At 3:30 PM, the sun on California Avenue was already beginning to slant towards Stanford. The dog outside Zombie Café was lying under the white chair again. Three printouts lay on the table: Anthropic's Opus 4.7 release statement from April 16th, a transcript of Greg Brockman's GPT-5.5 presentation from April 23rd at OpenAI, and the DeepSeek V4 technical report, fresh from this morning, still damp with ink.

Within eight days, these three companies have laid their cards on the table for the second quarter of 2026.

Before finishing his coffee, Silicon Valley's Alan Walker clarified things. No benchmark hype, no claiming any model "feels better," no press releases.

Breaking it down from first principles—technology, chips, price, audience, strategy, ecosystem—where will these three companies stand in 2026? Who is leading, who is defending their position, and who is upsetting the table?

01 Open Source vs. Closed Source—The Fundamental Path of This Battle

DeepSeek has simultaneously released two models, V4-Pro and V4-Flash, under the standard MIT License, with weight directly equivalent to Hugging Face. Anyone can download, fine-tune, and commercially use them.

02 Model Architecture—The three companies are not on the same path

V4-Pro 1.6T total parameters / 49B activation; V4-Flash 284B total parameters / 13B activation. 1M context is the factory default. The core architecture is hybrid attention (CSA + HCA interleaved) + Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections + Muon optimizer + FP4 training. In the 1M token scenario, V4-Pro's single-token inference FLOPs are only 27% of V3.2's, and the KV cache is only 10%. V4-Flash is even more aggressive—FLOPs are reduced to 10%, and the KV cache is reduced to 7%.

03  Underlying Chips—Today's Most Underrated News

Mainstream English media headlines are all about V4's benchmark. Wrong. The line that truly changes the game today is—part of V4's training was done on Huawei Ascend.
...

Claude and GPT-5.5 all run on NVIDIA H100/H200/B200 + Google TPU + Anthropic is building its own Trainium 2 stack. There is no other way, and no other supplier. This is a barrier, and also a single point of failure. If Nvidia raises prices or its production capacity can't keep up, both companies will suffer. DeepSeek now has an independent supply chain—they have another card to play. src="https://mmbiz.qpic.cn/sz_mmbiz_png/UwCaMkyCibice7uvkuMnzTeTyndz0Ga3SQcRj2brsjvAptFGCGxgMpQ7Ctvvf7cicU5WDpLWNnHzs5LicbyS F41RJsOCRzyQlER0lcrW1ZpGP8E/640?wx_fmt=png&from=appmsg&watermark=1&tp=webp&wxfrom=5&wx_lazy=1#imgIndex=6" _="" alt="image" data-report-img-idx="6" data-fail="0">

05 API Pricing—How Much Smartphone Can You Buy for One Dollar

Let's spread out the price list and take a look.

Let's spread out the price list and take a look.

06The Real Economics of Millions of Contexts

All three companies have reached 1M context. On the surface, it seems like they are neck and neck.

However—being able to do it and being able to do it cheaply are two different things.

V4-Pro scored 83.5 points on the MRCR long text retrieval benchmark, surpassing Gemini-3.1-Pro's 76.3, but lagging behind Claude Opus 4.6's 92.9. CorpusQA achieved 62% on 1M tokens, surpassing Gemini 3.1 Pro's 53.8%. Retrieval accuracy was 94% at 128K, 82% at 512K, and 66% at 1M.

While not number one in absolute terms, it's number one in open source, and the first to make 1M the default. Claude Opus 4.7's 1M context doesn't incur a price increase for long contexts—a significant advantage of Anthropic. GPT-5.5 also does. However, a problem arises: the cost difference between the three inference units is tenfold, and this gap is amplified tenfold in long context scenarios. Let's do a simple calculation. Analyzing a legal document with 500K tokens: Opus 4.7 costs $2.5 just for input, and $3-4 for output; GPT-5.5 is similar; V4-Pro is about $1. If this workflow runs 10,000 times a day, the annualized cost difference is several million to over ten million US dollars. The biggest bottleneck for medium-sized enterprises running agentic analysis workloads is the cost of long contexts, and V4-Pro effectively eliminates this bottleneck.

07 Encoding and Agent Capabilities—Three Players Each Guard Their Own Side

By laying out the benchmark table, this round is very clear.

08Target Audience—Three Companies Targeting Three Completely Different Wallets

A quick glance at Anthropic's client list reveals its direction: PayPal, Hex, Devin, Factory, Ramp, Notion, GitHub Copilot, Stripe, Block—all fintech and enterprise SaaS companies. These companies have two things in common: plenty of money and zero tolerance for errors. Opus 4.7's $5/$25 pricing, security audits, compliance narratives, Bedrock/Vertex AI/Foundry multi-cloud deployments—all of this is aimed at clients with procurement processes that last six months, three-year contracts, and annual payments of millions of dollars. On Forge Global, Anthropic's valuation exceeded $1 trillion, surpassing OpenAI's $880 billion; capital is buying into this story of "enterprise customer density."


09 Security and Cyber ​​Offense/Defense Stances—Three Companies' Vastly Different Attitudes Towards "Models as Weapons"

Anthropic released Project Glasswing in early April. Opus 4.7 is the first production model with built-in "automatic detection and rejection of high-risk cybersecurity requests." Anthropic itself stated bluntly in its technical report—offensive cyber capabilities were intentionally suppressed during training. Its CyberGym score of 73.1 is almost identical to Opus 4.6 (73.8), indicating a policy choice rather than a capability ceiling. Mythos Preview achieves 83.1 on the same benchmark, but it's only available to 12 partners, and even this list itself is confidential—it was leaked a few days ago (a Discord community guessed the URL), and Anthropic even released an incident report.

10 Market Strategy—Three Completely Different Bets, But Only One Will Be the Biggest

DeepSeek aims to be the Linux of the AI ​​world.

Democratizing global AI infrastructure through open source + extreme cost + domestically produced chips. Once every country, every company, and every developer is running on your architecture—you won't make money from license fees, you'll collect taxes from the value of your ecosystem. Today, Hugging Face will have a huge number of downloads; tomorrow, every domestic chip manufacturer's SDK will be adapted to DeepSeek by default; the day after tomorrow, every new generation of AI developers will start their first line of code with `from deepseek import...`. Linus Torvalds played this game twenty years ago, and Liang Wenfeng is playing the same game today.

data.title: Google spends $40 billion to secure Anthropic: A two-horse race in AI is taking shape.
data.description:
Google will invest up to $40 billion in Anthropic, further deepening the partnership between the two companies, who are both partners and rivals in the artificial intelligence race. Anthropic announced on Friday that Google has committed $10 billion in cash immediately at its latest valuation of $380 billion. Google will invest an additional $30 billion after Anthropic achieves performance milestones, while also significantly expanding Anthropic's computing power. Anthropic is a key customer for Google's chips and cloud services, which Google is aggressively expanding to compensate for the aging growth of its core revenue stream—search advertising. Google Cloud will provide Anthropic with 5 gigawatts of computing power over the next five years, starting in 2027, with potential additional gigawatts to be added later. This agreement is a further expansion of the cooperation agreement signed earlier this month between Anthropic, Google, and Broadcom. Google's TPU is one of the most competitive alternatives to Nvidia chips, and in this industry with extremely high computing power demands, TPUs are a scarce and valuable resource for Anthropic and other AI developers. This investment marks a fundamental restructuring of the competitive landscape of the AI ​​industry. For the past two years, the leading AI teams have been defined as the "Big Three"—OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic—standing in a three-way balance. Now, this narrative has ended. Listing Anthropic's funding rounds over the past six months reveals an intriguing reality: Amazon: $5 billion in cash, capped at $25 billion, plus 5 gigawatts of Trainium computing power and a $100 billion AWS procurement contract; Google: $10 billion in cash, capped at $40 billion, plus 5 gigawatts of TPU computing power; Nvidia: capped at $10 billion, plus 1 gigawatt of GPU supply; Microsoft: capped at $5 billion, and Anthropic procures $30 billion worth of computing power from Azure. Four top Silicon Valley players are all listed as shareholders of Anthropic. Their cumulative computing power commitment exceeds 11 gigawatts—equivalent to the power generation of 10 nuclear power plants. The landscape has evolved from a "three-way split" to a two-way battle between the Anthropic camp and OpenAI. The era of the "Big Three" is over. 02. Core Divergence: Large Models + ASIC, or Large Models + GPU? Behind this restructuring, a clearer technological divide is emerging. Anthropic follows a "large-scale model + ASIC" approach. Google's TPU and Amazon's Trainium are both application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) designed specifically for AI workloads. Anthropic's future computing power will primarily be supported by these two ASIC systems. One reason Google is willing to invest $40 billion in Anthropic is precisely this – Google's capital expenditure plan this year is a staggering $185 billion, with a large portion allocated to data centers and TPU production capacity. If TPUs don't have large customers to absorb them, they become the most expensive inventory. Anthropic serves as both a gateway to enterprise customers and the best ballast for Google's TPU production capacity. OpenAI, on the other hand, follows a "large-scale model + GPU" approach. OpenAI's core computing power comes from the Stargate project, deeply integrated with NVIDIA—a massive infrastructure plan with a target scale of $500 billion, primarily based on NVIDIA GPUs. The advantage of this approach lies in the mature NVIDIA GPU ecosystem and complete software stack; however, the problem is the long implementation cycle. Stargate is expected to reach full production capacity around 2029, while the physical progress of the first Texas data center remains slow. The underlying logic of the two approaches is fundamentally different. The ASIC approach has the advantages of high energy efficiency and lower cost per unit of computing power, but it requires high customization and has relatively limited ecosystem flexibility; the GPU approach has the advantages of strong versatility and a broader developer ecosystem, but its power consumption and cost remain high. The current competitive landscape is, to some extent, a proxy war between the ASIC and GPU camps: Google and Amazon are using Anthropic to validate and promote their own ASIC chips, while Nvidia is consolidating its GPU dominance in AI infrastructure through OpenAI and Stargate. Google's Calculation: Rather than a direct confrontation, it's better to leverage external forces. Google's willingness to invest 40 billion in its nominal competitors can be understood by looking at three figures. First, Anthropic's annualized revenue surpassed $30 billion in March 2026, compared to only about $1 billion at the beginning of 2025—a 30-fold increase in just one year. Claude Code has expanded beyond the programmer community, fully opening up enterprise channels, and its B2B clients have spread from startups to Fortune 500 companies. Second, Anthropic's implied valuation in the secondary market is approaching $1 trillion, and it continues to suppress Gemini's similar products in the AI ​​enterprise market's developer share. Third, Google clearly possesses DeepMind, Gemini, and the world's largest TPU cluster, yet for over two years since Gemini's release, its market share in the enterprise AI market has been consistently suppressed by Claude. Instead of continuing a direct confrontation, it's better to leverage existing strengths. Google's investment in Anthropic was a "buy if you can't afford to lose" hedging strategy: if Anthropic wins in the enterprise AI market, Google can at least receive equity returns; if Gemini succeeds, Google wins on both fronts; if Gemini fails, TPU shipments are secured, and the AI ​​business can also gain a foothold through Anthropic.

04The Most Awkward Role: OpenAI

Anthropic's computing power problem has been solved, but OpenAI's own computing power problem has begun to stand out.

Anthropic received two multi-billion dollar injections from Amazon and Google within four days, securing a computing power commitment of over 11 gigawatts. OpenAI has been coordinating the Stargate project for nearly a year, with frequent rumors of funding gaps. SoftBank's promised funds are still arriving in installments, and Microsoft is no longer OpenAI's exclusive computing power supplier.

A deeper problem is that OpenAI's traditional advantages are being eroded. While the GPT series still leads in single-point performance, Claude Code continues to expand its market share in programming scenarios and Claude in enterprise applications. Meanwhile, Microsoft, a core ally of OpenAI, has quietly appeared on Anthropic's investor list—an extreme version of "hedging": Azure, which supports OpenAI, is also a source of computing power for Anthropic. If Anthropic already has four powerful backers—cloud giants (Amazon, Google), GPU giants (Nvidia), and Microsoft—OpenAI needs to find another strategic partner of equal caliber from sovereign wealth funds and cloud giants as soon as possible—and quickly. Benchmark scores can no longer determine the outcome of the AI ​​industry. The real arena in this reshuffling is a three-way battle between cash, computing power, and ecosystem. The moment Google invested 40 billion, the outcome of the first round was already decided.

data.title: Nine projects raised $53.3 million; Pharos raised $10 million.
data.description:
This week, nine blockchain startups completed funding rounds, raising a total of $53.3 million.

Largest Funding Round This Week

Pharos raised $10 million in a public sale funding round (specific investors undisclosed).

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications. It supports EVM compatibility, allowing Ethereum dApp developers to use familiar toolchains while benefiting from Pharos features such as 1-second final confirmation, reduced storage costs, and enhanced security provided through the AsyncBFT consensus mechanism.

Pharos aims to drive innovation in DeFi, Real-World Assets (RWA), Decentralized Physical Infrastructure (DePIN), and cross-chain interoperability by providing a unified account system across multiple virtual machines.

Pharos aims to drive innovation in DeFi, Real-World Assets (RWA), Decentralized Physical Infrastructure (DePIN), and cross-chain interoperability by providing a unified account system across multiple virtual machines.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications ... is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized applications.

Pharos is a deeply parallel Layer-1 blockchain network designed for high-speed, scalable, and decentralized

Pre-seed and Seed Investment Rounds

3F raised $4 million in its seed round, with investors including Maven 11 Capital*, F-Prime Capital, Metalayer Ventures, GSR Markets LTD, Susquehanna Crypto, Gate Ventures, Pivot Global, Komorebi Collective, and Serotonin.

3F is a global DeFi yield network that connects institutional-grade real-world assets (RWA) with on-chain finance. Built on Morpho, the project allows users to access curated RWA vaults, leverage, and transparently manage positions on-chain, while stablecoin lenders can earn yields through asset-backed lending strategies.

3F provides scalable, capital-efficient alternative asset exposure with global accessibility and composability.

3F offers scalable, capital-efficient alternative asset exposure with global accessibility and composability.

3F

Other Investment Rounds RealGo raised $3.5 million in a funding round (specific round undisclosed) from investors including Animoca Brands, Cogitent Ventures, x21 Digital, Notch Ventures, and Becker Ventures. RealGo is a Web3 AR mobile game that transforms real-world locations into interactive playgrounds through AI, AR, and location-based services. Players can explore global landmarks, capture meme-style characters, hunt for treasure, and engage in battles. The entire system operates within a SocialFi-driven economy. The platform integrates NFTs, token rewards, and a marketplace, enabling game assets to be owned and traded. RealGo connects Web2 and Web3 users through immersive exploration, social interaction, and a player governance economy by combining meme culture with blockchain gaming. Its native token is RT. Cluster Protocol raised $5 million in a funding round (specific round undisclosed) from investors including dao5, Paper Ventures, JPEG Trading, Mapleblock Capital, Fairum Ventures, Maven Capital, and Prim3 Capital. Cluster Protocol is a "computation proof protocol" and open-source community for decentralized AI models. It uses cryptography and federated learning to enable secure model training using private data, ensuring data locality and privacy protection. Its features include decentralized datasets, collaborative model training, and a mechanism for earning passive income by contributing computing power. Cluster Protocol aims to popularize GPU access, promote decentralized data sharing, lower the computational barrier, and simplify the monetization process for developers. ILITY raised $2 million in a private funding round from investors including Animoca Brands and The DuckDao. ILITY Network is a privacy-first Layer-1 blockchain designed for cross-chain privacy data verification. It uses zero-knowledge proofs (ZKP) to allow users to prove cross-chain assets, holdings, transaction history, and on-chain behavior without exposing wallet addresses or underlying data. This builds a unified, self-governing identity system that aggregates on-chain activities scattered across multiple chains while maintaining complete privacy and anonymity. BetHog (FanDuel) raised $10 million in its Series A funding round, with investors including Will Ventures*, RockawayX*, Phoenix Capital Ventures (PCV), 6th Man Ventures (6MV), Bullpen Capital, and Advancit Capital. FanDuel is a leading online sports betting and daily fantasy sports platform. BetHog is a new crypto casino and sports betting platform launched by the founder of FanDuel, offering unique games and a player-centric experience. Built on the Solana blockchain, the platform supports PvP casino games, including the exclusive “BetHog Originals,” providing innovative gameplay for crash and mines-like games. Its pioneering PvP feature allows users to play with well-known streamers. BetHog supports SOL and is compatible with Bitcoin, Ethereum, and USDT, targeting the growing crypto gaming market. Hata raised $8 million in its Series A funding round (investor: ByBit*). Hata is a global cryptocurrency exchange that allows users to buy, sell, and trade crypto assets using fiat currency. The platform offers a user-friendly interface, supports a variety of digital assets, and provides secure trading and liquidity for both novice and professional traders.

Kaio raised $8 million in strategic funding from investors including Tether, Systemic Ventures, Further Ventures, and Laser Digital.

KAIO is a sovereign AppChain protocol designed specifically for Real-World Assets (RWA), supporting compliant, liquid, and cross-chain tokenized fund flows.

It provides the infrastructure for issuing, redeeming, and transferring RWA-backed tokens, enabling accredited investors to subscribe via fiat currency or stablecoins.

KAIO connects traditional finance with DeFi, enabling secure on-chain access to cross-chain token assets and driving interoperability and regulatory compliance alignment.

Octra raised $2.8 million in a public sale funding round (specific investors undisclosed).

Octra is a blockchain network focused on fully homomorphic encryption (FHE), providing cryptographic tools for secure mathematical operations in an isolated execution environment.

The platform supports the development of smart contracts using OCaml, AST, ReasonML, and C++, and provides open-source libraries such as Hypergraph to enhance compatibility. Octra uses an extended version of IrminDB for data management by validators.

data.title: Top 10 Most Influential People in the Cryptocurrency Industry
data.description:
Author: James Allan; Translator: Plain Language Blockchain

Blockchain is more than just an industry—it's a community. Like any community, it's shaped by shared rituals, stories, hierarchies, and its leaders. It's often defined by those figures recognized and sought after for direction and inspiration by its collective members. Who are these people in the blockchain world? What have they built, and how have they impacted this space?

This article attempts to answer this question—whether for newcomers trying to find their footing or for former participants seeking a more radical perspective. Here, I outline the ten most influential thinkers in the blockchain space as of 2026. The names on the detailed list may evolve over time, but the purpose remains the same: to capture a microcosm of this dominance and preserve a piece of history as the ecosystem continues to grow and change.

1. Satoshi Nakamoto

The story begins with Satoshi Nakamoto—a mysterious figure who showed the world the first truly decentralized form of currency and then disappeared. The system Satoshi Nakamoto created—Bitcoin—became the cornerstone upon which the entire crypto ecosystem is built.

Satoshi Nakamoto's achievements were not only economic but also profound technological breakthroughs. By designing Bitcoin, he solved the long-standing double-spending problem—the challenge of preventing digital currency from being copied and spent more than once without relying on a centralized institution.

Satoshi Nakamoto's achievements were not only economic but also profound technological breakthroughs. By designing Bitcoin, he solved the long-standing double-spending problem—the challenge of preventing digital currency from being copied and spent more than once without relying on a centralized institution.

By doing so, he unlocked a completely new model of trust: trust rooted in code, learning, and cryptography, rather than institutional trust. No interviews, no public identity—only code, writings, and a network that is still running today. In the crypto community, he remains a mythical figure: both founder and native, a true legend. 2. Vitalik Buterin After Bitcoin gained mainstream adoption, blockchain was largely understood as a way to securely and transparently track the flow of funds in a centralized system. But some saw a much broader vision. Vitalik Buterin realized that decentralized networks wouldn't necessarily hinder finance—they would evolve into the infrastructure of the internet itself. His core idea was simple yet revolutionary: what if blockchains could run any type of program, not just record payments? This idea gave birth to Ethereum—a platform where developers can configure code that executes automatically on a global network. This created the concept often referred to as a "world computer." Thousands of nodes running the same code and aggregating consensus on the results eliminate the need for centralized control. The code deployed on Ethereum is called a smart contract—a self-executing program stored on the blockchain. While initially underestimated, they ushered in a new era: software that runs autonomously built in. As these systems become increasingly complex and play a larger role in digital infrastructure, it's worth remembering where this transformation originated—from the vision of Ethereum and Vitalik Buterin. 3. Gavin Wood Gavin Wood, as a co-founder of Ethereum, worked alongside Vitalik Buterin to help translate his vision into concrete systems. He wrote Ethereum's Yellow Paper, the official technical specification of the protocol, and created **Solidity**, currently the most widely used programming language for writing smart protocols. He also popularized the **Web3** protocol, a term that has since become synonymous with decentralized networks. But his work clearly stopped at Ethereum. His vision foresaw a multi-chain future, leading to the creation of Polkadot. Polkadot's core is built on the idea that blockchains shouldn't exist in isolation—they should be able to communicate and share security. To achieve this, it introduced parachains: custom blockchains connected to a central chain. Because interoperability is built into the design, these parachains can intersect, solving one of the biggest challenges in the space—waste disposal. Importantly, Wood played a role in co-creating Parity Technologies, which developed Substrate, a framework also used for building custom blockchains. Substrate makes blockchain development more standardized and scalable, much like modern frameworks do for web development, reducing the labor force for engineers building in this space. In short, Gavin Wood is one of the few figures who has contributed to every aspect of the blockchain stack—theory, implementation, and ecosystem design. Please respect his name. 4. Silvio Micali Silvio Micali, often compared to Alan Turing, has had an unavoidable influence on the field of modern cryptography. He will introduce rigorous scientific thinking into blockchain, helping to transform it into an infrastructure form that complements its mathematical foundations and security. Micali was already one of the world's leading cryptographers when blockchain first emerged. He was nominated for the Turing Award in 2012 for co-inventing zero-knowledge proofs—a groundbreaking concept that allows verification of true information without revealing the underlying data. The impending impact of this concept is particularly relevant given that trust and verification are fundamental properties prior to blockchain systems. Silvio Mikalli's most significant technical contribution to blockchain is Pure Proof-of-Stake (PPoS), a consensus mechanism designed to achieve broad participation with extremely low stake. Unlike energy-intensive systems like Bitcoin or traditional PoS models that may concentrate influence among a large number of token holders, PPoS relies on cryptographic randomness to fairly and securely select participants. Mikalli implemented this approach in the blockchain protocol algorithm he created. 5. Eli Ben-Sasson Eli Ben-Sasson is a key figure in the blockchain field because he helped bring scalable zero-knowledge systems to real-world applications, making networks faster, more efficient, and more privacy-preserving. He co-invented zk-STARKs, a type of zero-knowledge proof built on the foundational ideas of cryptographers like Silvio Micarri. These proofs allow verification of computational correctness without revealing the underlying data, representing a dual breakthrough in scalability and privacy. Ben-Sassoon's extensive work has validated Ethereum's Layer 2 scaling solutions, in which a large number of transactions can be processed off-chain and then verified on-chain using concise cryptographic proofs. Therefore, his contributions are a major reason why many believe the future of blockchain will be "ZK-driven," with zero-knowledge technology serving as the framework for scalable and secure centralized systems. 6. Charles Hoskinson Charles Hoskinson is an original co-founder of Ethereum. He ultimately chose one of the two paths of closure and departure, and went on to create Cardano, a platform built on exotic philosophies. Cardano is a “research-first” blockchain designed to address perceived shortcomings in early blockchains like Bitcoin and Ethereum, such as scalability, governance, and long-term sustainability. Charles is a compelling and controversial, yet influential figure. While Silvio Micali established modern proof-of-stake systems, Hoskinson helped popularize and advance them through Cardano. Charles’s most significant contribution to the field is pushing “governance” to the core design priority of any protocol. In other words, he elevated blockchain from a financial and computational system to a foundation for governance. While early networks like Bitcoin and even Ethereum had triangular or off-chain governance, Hoskinson emphasized formal on-chain governance mechanisms as a civic function. Hoskinson demonstrated that blockchain can function as a **governance machine**. This idea alone is enough to place him firmly in the ranks of blockchain pioneers. 7. Yonatan Sompolinsky Yonatan Sompolinsky is a key figure in pushing blockchain technology from the traditional linear architecture to a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** structure. Instead of relying on the traditional model of connecting blocks to a single unit, he provided a graph-based approach that achieved **true hardwareization**—unlocking higher throughput without requiring sharding or sacrificing centralization. By successfully implementing the BlockDAG architecture in the protocol Kaspa, which he founded, Jonathan Sompolinski proved that this Proof-of-Work (PoW) system can scale efficiently—a significant milestone for the industry. Furthermore, Jonathan and his team (particularly Michael Sutton) introduced the concept of verifiable procedures (vProgs). These procedures act as instant locks, allowing transactions to be executed only if verified with zero-knowledge proofs. This innovation brings enhanced flexibility and scalability to UTXO architecture systems without compromising the security model. History will likely remember Jonathan Sompolinski as a pioneer of next-generation blockchain design—a design that redefines how scalability, security, and decentralization can coexist. 8. Changpeng Zhao (Changpeng Zhu) Changpeng Zhao, often referred to as "CZ," has consistently been one of the most influential figures in the growth of the cryptocurrency industry. His influence largely stems from his founding of Binance in 2017, which rapidly grew into the world's largest cryptocurrency exchange by trading volume. Binance significantly lowered the barrier to entry by providing an easily accessible, well-defined, and highly liquid trading platform. This made the cryptocurrency market accessible to millions of users globally and accelerated the expansion of the overall market. In this way, he helped transform cryptocurrency from a niche technology into a mainstream financial instrument—especially in emerging markets where traditional financial infrastructure could be established. 9. Brian Armstrong Brian Armstrong, a former Airbnb engineer, founded Coinbase with the aim of making it simple and accessible for ordinary users to buy and sell Bitcoin. Over time, Coinbase has expanded its range of supported assets and become a key driver in pushing cryptocurrencies into the mainstream—particularly in the United States. In 2021, Coinbase achieved a major milestone, becoming the first major cryptocurrency company to go public through a direct listing on Nasdaq. Armstrong will likely be remembered for building one of the most trusted gateways to cryptocurrency and for helping to bridge the gap between blockchain technology and traditional finance. 10. Michael Saylor Whether you like him or hate him, Michael Saylor has undoubtedly left a lasting mark on the crypto industry. He is one of the most influential figures driving institutional adoption of Bitcoin, and his beliefs have translated into billions of dollars in capital inflows today. In addition to holding Bitcoin in the national treasury, Saylor co-founded MicroStrategy, which became the first major publicly traded company to adopt Bitcoin on a large scale. Thaler's contribution lies not in building new technologies, but in **accumulating and guiding capital**—helping to position Bitcoin as a core asset in modern financial strategies. The Blockchain Trilemma. Blockchain is still a young and rapidly evolving industry. As the field matures, it will become clearer who are the temporary passersby and who are the truly enduring players. Aside from Satoshi Nakamoto, the voices of these individuals are prominently featured here. It will be very interesting to observe who fades away and who continues to expand their influence over time. Regardless of the future, their impact on the industry is already firmly established.

data.title: The White House gunman was arrested; only nine public blockchains have earned over $100,000 in fees in the past seven days.
data.description:
Headline

▌Trump says gunman involved in White House Correspondents' Association dinner has been arrested

On April 26, US President Trump said on social media on the 25th that one of the gunmen involved in the "security incident" at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner had been arrested.

(Xinhua News Agency) According to Nansen data, only 9 public blockchains earned over $100,000 in fees in the past 7 days, with only 5 of them earning over $1 million. Ethereum topped the list with $10.81 million in fees, followed by Tron ($7.15 million), Solana ($3.43 million), BNB Chain ($2.48 million), Bitcoin ($1.42 million), Near ($858,500), Base ($723,500), Polygon ($448,900), and Arbitrum ($163,300). As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $78,214.63, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $2,356.41, up 1.7% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $641.52, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $88.03, up 0.9% in the last 24 hours. DOGE price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $1.45, 24-hour change +0.8%; TRX price is $0.3246, 24-hour change +0.5%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; DOGE price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $1.45, 24-hour change +0.8%; TRX price is $0.3246, 24-hour change +0.5%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; WLFI price is $0.07694, 24-hour change +2.1%; XRP price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +1.3%; XRP price is $0.09984, 24-hour change +0.8 ... HYPE price is $42.37, a 24-hour change of +3.1%. Policy: Trump says he will not allow banks to undermine the cryptocurrency market. On April 26, CoinDesk, citing sources at a Trump cryptocurrency event, reported that Trump stated he would not allow banks to undermine the cryptocurrency market.

      Blockchain Applications

      ▌Litecoin Discloses Zero-Day Vulnerability Causing DoS Attack and Abnormal MWEB Transactions; Issue Now Fixed

      On April 26th, Litecoin disclosed on the X platform that a recent zero-day vulnerability had led to a DoS attack, affecting the operation of major mining pools. Mining nodes that had not updated in time allowed an invalid MWEB (MimbleWimble Extension Block) transaction to be executed, allowing the related tokens to be withdrawn to a third-party DEX. The Litecoin network rolled back these invalid transactions through a 13-block reorg, confirming that they would not be included in the main chain. During this period, all valid transactions were unaffected. The vulnerability has now been fully fixed, and the network has returned to normal operation.

        Cryptocurrency

        ▌Bitwise Submits Application for New Prediction Market ETF Related to Economic Recession and Tech Industry Layoffs on Friday Morning

        On April 26, according to Bloomberg ETF analyst James Seyffart on the X platform, Bitwise submitted an application for a new prediction market ETF related to economic recession and tech industry layoffs on Friday morning.

        ▌An entity transferred 34.61 million ASTER to Aster early this morning, worth $22.94 million

        On April 26, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, an entity that withdrew 50.1 million ASTER from Aster last September transferred 34.61 million ASTER ($22.94 million) to Aster early this morning. ASTER subsequently dropped by 5% ($0.66 → $0.63). Currently, the entity's on-chain address still holds 24.25 million ASTER ($15.82 million).

        ▌An entity transferred 34.61 million ASTER to Aster early this morning, worth $22.94 million

        ▌Balancer attackers have exchanged 21,000 ETH for 617.43 BTC in the past three days

        On April 26, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, Balancer attackers have exchanged 21,000 ETH for 617.43 BTC in the past three days, worth $48.72 million. The attacker currently holds 1,000 ETH, worth $2.32 million, and may sell further.

        ▌RookieXBT linked wallet spends 25,000 USDC to buy 1.89 million SPIKE

        On April 26, according to Lookonchain monitoring, RookieXBT linked wallet spent 25,000 USDC to buy 1.89 million SPIKE.

        ▌20 million RAVE tokens were withdrawn from Bitget to 8 on-chain wallets by RAVE dealers 8 hours ago

        On April 26, according to on-chain analyst Yu Jin, 8 hours ago, 20 million RAVE tokens (US$16.78 million) were withdrawn from Bitget to 8 on-chain wallets by RAVE dealers.

        Important Economic Developments

        ▌Trump: Never Considered Backing Down on Ceasefire

        On April 26, US President Trump stated: I have never considered backing down on the ceasefire issue.

        When asked about Iran's enriched uranium, Trump said, "They offered a lot of conditions, but it wasn't enough." He stated, "The whole agreement isn't complicated." Trump reiterated that Iran must never possess nuclear weapons. Disclosure documents show Trump purchased at least $51 million in bonds in March. According to financial disclosure documents released Saturday, April 26, US President Trump purchased at least $51 million in bonds across multiple industries in March. According to forms released by the US Government Ethics Office, Trump conducted 175 financial transactions last month. These forms did not list the specific amounts for each transaction, only providing the range. The disclosed assets were mostly municipal bonds issued by states, counties, school districts, and other entities affiliated with government agencies or public-private partnerships. His 26 largest transactions, ranging from $1 million to $5 million, were primarily municipal bonds or U.S. Treasury bonds, although two of them involved the purchase of corporate bonds from Wieselhaus and General Motors. He also invested in an exchange-traded fund that tracks a high-yield bond index. Trump purchased corporate bonds in the energy, technology, healthcare, and financial services sectors, with issuers including Constellation Energy, Occidental Petroleum, Broadcom, Nvidia, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, and Wall Street banks Citigroup, Goldman Sachs, and JPMorgan Chase, as well as Boeing. Trump's total bond purchases across all asset classes amounted to approximately $161 million.

        ▌Iranian Foreign Minister to Visit Pakistan Again

        On April 26, the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA) reported on the 25th that, according to a plan released by the Iranian Foreign Ministry, Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi will visit Pakistan again after concluding his visit to Oman and before heading to Russia.

        (Xinhua News Agency) On April 26, according to Iranian sources, Iranian Deputy Speaker of the Islamic Republic of Iran, Nikozadeh, stated that Iran's Supreme Leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, has explicitly ordered that the Strait of Hormuz must not return to its pre-war state. (CCTV News) Golden Encyclopedia Can Bitcoin Still Be Called "Digital Gold" Amid Geopolitical Crises? Every time a geopolitical crisis occurs, gold prices rise while Bitcoin prices plummet. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been substantiated by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Referral rewards in cryptocurrency projects do not equate to pyramid schemes or other crimes: it depends on where the money comes from.
data.description:
If a cryptocurrency project possesses specific characteristics, combined with the current domestic policy of severely cracking down on cryptocurrency speculation, there is a significant criminal risk of it being classified as the crime of organizing or leading pyramid schemes.

In the previous article "Caught in a Cryptocurrency Pyramid Scheme, the Involved Projects Are Usually These Four Types, Attorney Shao has already outlined the typical models and variations of cryptocurrency projects involved in pyramid schemes.

However, the concept of Web3 iterates rapidly, and models emerge endlessly. In judicial practice, there are also instances where judicial organs, due to their unfamiliarity with Web3 project models, have mistakenly judged some Web3 projects with genuine business logic as pyramid scheme crimes.

This article aims to explore: What kind of Web3 project models should not be considered pyramid scheme crimes? Where exactly is the lawyer's room for defense? This article will analyze this issue using specific cases. Let's look at a case first. Machael and others built a cryptocurrency platform and issued cryptocurrency X. The project model is as follows: For every transaction of X tokens by a downline member, the platform collects a certain percentage of the service fee, and at the same time, 20% of the service fee is awarded to the upline who developed that downline member as a referral reward. In this case, do Machael and others constitute the crime of organizing and leading a pyramid scheme? From a formal perspective, the platform pays referral rewards to its upline based on the transactions of its downline, which seems to meet the formal requirement of "using the number of people recruited as the basis for remuneration," and there seems to be little controversy in determining that it constitutes a pyramid scheme crime? However, this judgment is clearly too hasty. Distinguishing between pyramid scheme crimes and administrative violations of team-based remuneration: The key is to look at the source of the upline's profits. According to the 2013 "Opinions of the Supreme People's Court, the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and the Ministry of Public Security on Several Issues Concerning the Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Organizing and Leading Pyramid Schemes," simple "team-based remuneration" pyramid schemes that aim to sell goods and use sales performance as the basis for remuneration are not treated as crimes. This regulation establishes the core distinction between pyramid scheme crimes and administrative violations in practice: What is the source of the upline's profits—the principal of the downline or the platform's actual operating income? If it comes from the downline's principal—essentially "robbing Peter to pay Paul," using money from later participants to pay for earlier ones' profits—it's a typical Ponzi scheme and constitutes a suspected pyramid scheme crime. However, if it comes from profits generated by the platform through genuine business operations—then it can be argued that the team-based compensation model is an administrative violation, not a crime. Therefore, the starting point for lawyers handling such cases is to determine the source and nature of the funds for upline rewards in the specific model of the project in question. Cryptocurrency projects involved in pyramid schemes: How to determine if a platform has genuine operating income? Returning to Machael's case at the beginning. To prove that the platform does not constitute a pyramid scheme, it is necessary to combine the specific circumstances of the project and find a plausible business logic—proving that the platform's reward funds for upline members are earned by itself, not stolen from downline members. This needs to be considered in two scenarios. Test 1: Besides reselling, what else can tokens be used for? If the sole purpose of the token within the platform is to resell it to the next participant, or to exchange it for USDT in a self-built "fake DEX" (where USDT is the initial investment of later entrants), then the token has no independent use value, the project has no real operating income, and the funds for launch rewards can only come from the initial investment of new users, making a Ponzi scheme undeniable. Conversely, if the token can be used to purchase goods or services with independent value within the application—NFT equipment, membership benefits, data services, game items, etc.—and the consumption proceeds can be traced back to the project's treasury, then the project has a factual basis to claim "real operating income." Test Two: Can you participate without buying the token? This is the key to determining whether purchasing cryptocurrency constitutes an "entry fee" for a pyramid scheme. On Web3 platforms, users often need to first exchange RMB for USDT, and then for the platform's token. Whether this cryptocurrency purchase qualifies as an entry fee depends on this: If, without purchasing the token, an account cannot be activated and a referral link cannot be generated, the purchase is forcibly linked to participation eligibility, thus risking being considered an entry fee. Conversely, if users can register for free and obtain initial tokens by completing tasks, but earning tokens is slower without purchasing them, the mandatory nature of the purchase is insufficient, and it should not be considered an entry fee. Taking Move-to-Earn projects as an example: Three qualitative results. Taking "Run-to-Earn" projects as an example, combining the two testing criteria mentioned above, the same project type may fall into three completely different categories in terms of legal nature. Tier 1: Not Constitutes Any Pyramid Scheme or Illegal Activities Users can use basic functions for free; purchasing NFT running shoes is merely an added-value option, not a mandatory entry barrier; referral rewards are based on the actual spending amount of the referred user (such as NFT royalty sharing), not the number of people recruited; tokens can be used for in-app purchases, buying items, and paying for services, demonstrating real-world usage scenarios; the project team has real operating income such as NFT royalties and advertising partnerships, and the launch rewards originate from this, not from new users' initial investment. —This model has neither a mandatory entry fee nor a tiered rebate structure, and therefore does not constitute any form of pyramid scheme or illegal activity. Second Tier: Constitutes a Team-Based Compensation-Based Administrative Violation, But Not a Crime. There is a tiered rebate structure where uplines can receive rewards from the consumption and purchasing behavior of downlines; however, rebates are calculated based on the sales performance (amount or quantity of NFTs purchased) of downlines, not on the number of people recruited; the project aims to sell NFTs/tokens, and there is a genuine flow of goods; there is no subjective intent to defraud. — This meets Article 7, Paragraph (3) of the "Regulations on Prohibiting Pyramid Schemes," and is a team-based compensation-based pyramid scheme, but not a crime. The third tier: Constitutes a pyramid scheme crime. This involves forcing participants to purchase expensive NFTs/tokens as an entry threshold; calculating rebates directly based on the number of people recruited, regardless of actual consumption; promising high-interest static returns, with funds originating from the principal of later recruits; tokens having no real consumption scenario, serving only as a pyramid scheme accounting tool; and having the subjective intent to defraud. —Simultaneously meeting the four elements of "entry fee + tiered structure + recruitment-based compensation + defrauding," this constitutes the crime of organizing and leading a pyramid scheme. One additional point: What does it mean if the project lacks a real consumption scenario? This is precisely the crux of most cryptocurrency pyramid schemes. If the platform's tokens, aside from being resold within the platform to the next participant, have no real consumption scenarios—if users buy tokens solely to wait for appreciation or obtain static returns—then the project lacks any real operating income, and the funds for referral rewards can only come from the principal of new users. Regardless of how the platform's internal reward rules are designed, the underlying structure of the entire model is a Ponzi scheme of "robbing Peter to pay Paul," making it difficult to change the fact that the project itself is a pyramid scheme. Defense Points: The following four points must be verifiable simultaneously. If the project team intends to claim that it does not constitute a pyramid scheme crime, or only an administrative violation, then the following four points must be verifiable simultaneously: The tokens have real consumption scenarios and can be used to purchase goods or services with independent value within the application; The consumption funds do indeed enter the project team's treasury, and the on-chain fund flow is traceable; The referral rewards originate from the project team's revenue, rather than being directly deducted from the principal of the downline; The reward is triggered upon completion of consumption, not upon purchase or staking of tokens. If evidence is missing or broken in any of the above stages, the risk of classifying it as a pyramid scheme crime will significantly increase. In conclusion, such cases involve the project's token economic design, on-chain fund flows, and verification of the authenticity of consumption scenarios. If judicial authorities are unfamiliar with Web3 business models, it can lead to deviations in the classification of related cases. Furthermore, the Web3 field itself iterates extremely rapidly; when each new model emerges, the corresponding judicial understanding is often still lacking. However, this also means that there is considerable room for defense in these types of cases. As a defense attorney, one needs to be familiar with the business model and operational logic of such projects to find an effective entry point.

data.title: ETFs see net inflows for six consecutive days: Is Bitcoin about to enter an $80,000 short squeeze?
data.description:
Author: Ma He, Foresight News

On April 23, Bitcoin was priced around $78,000, having broken through $79,000 yesterday, reaching a new high since February this year. ETH is still fluctuating around $2,400, while some altcoins have seen significant gains.

According to Coinglass data, in the past 24 hours, the total open interest liquidation data for contracts across the network reached $462 million, with short positions liquidated at $353 million. According to the latest data from CMC, the market fear and greed index has rebounded to 60, with sentiment remaining neutral.

Global risk assets continued their rebound momentum.

Global risk assets continued their rebound momentum.

... U.S. stocks hit new record highs again yesterday: the S&P 500 closed at 7,137.90 points, up 1.05% on the day; the Nasdaq Composite Index closed at 24,657.57 points, up 1.64%, a new record high; and the Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at 49,490.03 points, up 340.65 points or 0.69%. Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar index (DXY) stabilized at a high level around 98.61, fluctuating slightly from the previous day. Trump announced on Wednesday an extension of the ceasefire agreement. Coupled with strong first-quarter corporate earnings reports, U.S. stocks quickly recovered from the previous two days of consecutive corrections. On Wednesday, the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closed at record highs, with chip stocks rising for the 16th consecutive day, while oil prices surged in tandem, diverging from market optimism.
