Trump announced an extension of the ceasefire at the request of Pakistani mediators, stating that US-Iran peace talks could resume as early as Friday, but Iran subsequently denied the possibility of Friday negotiations. The Iranian president stated that he "welcomes dialogue and agreements," but criticized Trump for "contradictions in his words and actions." Iranian Parliament Speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Baqer Qalibaf stated that a comprehensive ceasefire is impossible if the blockade is not lifted. Oil prices surged on Wednesday, marking their third consecutive day of gains. WTI crude futures rebounded to levels seen before the breakdown of negotiations, while Brent crude approached $102. U.S. stocks closed higher on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 hitting a record high and the Nasdaq hitting a new all-time high, led by technology stocks. The S&P 500 technology sector rose about 2%, the best performing of the 11 sectors. Furthermore, according to the latest data from Polymarket, the market's probability of one Fed rate cut this year has risen to 30%. With expectations of easing tensions in the Middle East, the safe-haven appeal of the dollar has significantly weakened. Coupled with the return of market bets on a Fed rate cut this year, global funds are accelerating their withdrawal from dollar assets. Data shows that the US dollar index has fallen by about 2.3% from its high at the end of March, potentially marking its worst monthly performance since August last year. Wall Street institutions generally believe that this round of dollar weakness is essentially driven by a dual factor: the fading of safe-haven premiums and a shift in policy expectations. JPMorgan Chase has restarted its short-dollar strategy and turned bullish on risk currencies such as the Australian dollar; Bank of New York Mellon also pointed out that emerging market currencies have rebounded across the board, reflecting a significant recovery in global risk appetite. BTC spot ETFs have achieved net inflows for six consecutive days. From April 14th to 21st, the total daily net inflow into the ETF was positive for all six days.

Among them, the net inflow on April 17th reached a high of $663.91 million, the highest in recent times; April 14th and April 20th recorded net inflows of $411.5 million and $238.37 million respectively; April 15th contributed $186 million.

On the other hand, there were only 4 days of net outflows, and the outflow amount did not exceed $400 million on any single day.

As for Ethereum spot ETFs, since April 9th ​​of this year, they have achieved a rare 9 consecutive days of net inflows. On April 17th, a net inflow of 127 million was recorded in a single day, setting a new high for the month. Net outflows only occurred on four days this month. Regarding stablecoin data, according to DefiLlama, it has now risen to $320.6 billion, with a net inflow of $635 million over the past seven days. Subsequent Trends: Glassnode released a data chart stating that Bitcoin has recovered the $78,000 mark, with both spot demand and ETF inflows returning. Short positions, coupled with the accumulation of negative funding rates, create the potential for a short squeeze. However, excessively high realized profits and low volatility are sending cautionary signals, and upside potential around $80,000 faces resistance. Bitcoin tweeted that new sources of Bitcoin demand are gradually emerging. Strategy's continued buying provides relatively stable buying support for the market, and clearer signs of allocation-oriented fund inflows are beginning to appear. Specifically, Coinbase Premium continues to rebound, with the spot Bitcoin ETF seeing a net inflow of approximately $664 million in a single day, the highest level since mid-January. These signals all point in one direction: the demand structure is recovering. Corporate financial buying, ETF inflows, and US spot demand are working together to solidify support at price lows, and market participation is also rebounding, a significant departure from the environment of the previous correction phase. Combined with yesterday's analysis of stablecoin fund inflows, liquidity support is also gradually strengthening. These two lines of reasoning suggest the market may be gradually building a new trading range. This doesn't mean the price will rise linearly, but if the above trends continue, the probability of prices moving towards the upper limit of the range is increasing.

data.title: a16z Crypto: 9 Charts to Understand the Evolution of Stablecoins
data.description:
Author: a16z crypto; Translator: Jiahuan, ChainCatcher

Stablecoins have been searching for their place for years.

Initially, they were simply a trading instrument, a means of transferring dollars across exchanges. They then evolved into a savings instrument, held rather than consumed. Now, data points to a new direction: stablecoins are gradually becoming core financial infrastructure.

The following nine charts reveal the factors driving this trend.

Regulation Accelerated Market Growth

For much of the stablecoin development process, regulatory uncertainty limited institutional participation. Later, the GENIUS Act brought regulatory clarity. It didn't create the trend, but it did amplify it.

In the United States, the GENIUS Act established the first federal-level framework for stablecoin issuance. This shift is clearly visible in the data: before the act's passage, adjusted trading volumes had been rising for several consecutive quarters, and the growth accelerated further after its passage, reaching approximately $4.5 trillion in the first quarter of 2026.

European regulatory frameworks—the Crypto Asset Markets Act (MiCA)—tell a more complex story. When the act fully came into effect at the end of 2024, several major exchanges delisted USDT for compliance, leading to a surge in activity in non-USD stablecoins, exceeding $40 billion at one point.

Subsequently, trading volume stabilized above pre-MiCA benchmark levels, at approximately $15 billion to $25 billion per month. Regulation created a persistent market for non-USD stablecoins that was virtually nonexistent before.

Stablecoin business activity is growing

Perhaps the most noteworthy structural change lies in how people actually use stablecoins.

Stablecoin business activity is growing

The most noteworthy structural change may lie in how people actually use stablecoins.

In terms of raw transaction volume, the C2C category far surpasses all other categories: reaching 789.5 million transactions in 2025. However, consumer-to-business stablecoin transactions saw the fastest growth, reaching 284.6 million transactions in 2025, more than doubling (+128%) from 124.9 million in 2024. Data from stablecoin card infrastructure highlights this trend. The monthly collateral deposits for stablecoin card projects backed by Rain (including Etherfi Cash, Kast, Wallbit, etc.) grew from near zero in November 2024 to over $300 million per month by early 2026. While this is collateral supporting consumption, rather than direct consumption of stablecoins, the trajectory is remarkable: stablecoin commercial activity is on the rise. Stablecoin velocity of circulation is accelerating. The turnover rate of each dollar of stablecoin supply is increasing. Since the beginning of 2024, the velocity of stablecoins (the adjusted ratio of monthly transaction volume to circulating supply) has nearly doubled, climbing from 2.6x to 6x. This increased velocity of circulation means that demand for stablecoin transactions exceeds new issuance, and the existing supply is working harder to keep up with demand. This is a hallmark of a true payment network—the base money is being actually used, not just held. Stablecoin trading volume reflects more payment activity. If we exclude transactions, fund flows, and exchange mechanisms, which account for the bulk of stablecoin trading, the total amount of payments between different entities last year is still estimated at $350 billion to $550 billion. In terms of transaction volume, business-to-business (B2B) transactions dominate stablecoin payments (which is unsurprising given their size). However, other areas, such as direct consumer-to-consumer payments and transactions with merchants, are also expanding rapidly. Stablecoin payments are currently concentrated in specific regions. Geographically, stablecoin payment activity is unevenly distributed. Nearly two-thirds of the transaction volume comes from Asia, mainly concentrated in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Japan. North America accounts for about a quarter. Europe accounts for about 13%. Latin America and Africa combined account for only a very small portion, less than $1 billion. Beyond cross-border payments, it's about local currencies running on a global track. The development of non-USD stablecoins is not limited to Europe; it is also emerging in emerging markets, driven by various factors. Brazil is a prime example. BRLA (a stablecoin backed by the Brazilian real) saw its monthly transaction volume grow from near zero in early 2023 to approximately $400 million per month in early 2026. Access to the PIX Instant Payments Network fueled its adoption. While stablecoins are often described as cross-border tools, the proportion of cross-border activity has actually been declining, not increasing. Domestic transactions (stablecoin transfers within the same country) have grown from about half of total payments in early 2024 to nearly three-quarters in early 2026. What does this mean? Stablecoins have not only established themselves as remittance or foreign exchange tools, but have also become a local payment medium operating on a global infrastructure. Taking all these factors together, a clear picture emerges, though not as most people expected: many once thought stablecoins would focus entirely on cross-border transactions. Instead, they are becoming increasingly localized. While the US dollar remains the core fiat currency pegged to the vast majority of stablecoins, stablecoins are by no means simply an export of the US dollar. Non-dollar variants, such as euro-backed and Brazilian real-backed local currency stablecoins, are gaining popularity. While peer-to-peer (C2C) stablecoin transfers far outnumber other types of payment flows, an increasing number of use cases are shifting towards everyday consumer spending (C2B). Quarterly data provides further evidence that stablecoins are evolving into a universal payment infrastructure. They are designed for global reach but are becoming increasingly localized in practice. It is still in its early stages, but the form of this system is gradually becoming clearer.
